Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Martin Heidegger

1889 – 1976

Being-in-the-world: the existential phenomenology of a being for whom its own being is at issue.

A Heideggerian philosophy of mind has to begin with a warning: Heidegger does not offer a “theory of mind” in the modern analytic sense. He is not trying to explain mental states, qualia, cognition, representation, perception, or consciousness as a special domain of entities. His project is more radical and more destabilizing: he asks what kind of being we ourselves are such that anything like world, object, self, body, language, truth, illness, and “mind” can show up for us at all. That means his thought is not a psychology, but it is deeply relevant to psychology because it challenges the ontological assumptions under which psychology usually understands its object. Heidegger’s own project in Being and Time is the question of Being, pursued through an analytic of Dasein, the being for whom its own being is an issue; major reference sources summarize this as an account of human beings as beings-in-the-world, constituted through practical and social contexts rather than as Cartesian subjects facing an objective world.

The extracted doctrine can be stated like this: mindedness, for Heidegger, is not an inner, private, representational container; it is the temporally ecstatic, affectively attuned, embodied, socially normative, linguistically articulable openness of Dasein in which beings can matter and be encountered as what they are. “Mind” is therefore not a substance, not a ghostly faculty, not the brain considered in isolation, and not consciousness conceived as a self-contained field of mental contents. It is a derivative and somewhat misleading name for the broader phenomenon Heidegger calls disclosedness, being-in-the-world, care, and later the clearing.

1. Why Heidegger is not doing psychology, but why psychology matters to him

Heidegger’s early philosophical formation already involved an anti-psychologistic concern. His 1913 dissertation was on judgment and psychologism, and his later project continues to resist reducing logical, ontological, or phenomenological structures to empirical mental events. In Being and Time, he distinguishes the existential analytic of Dasein from anthropology, psychology, and biology, not because these sciences are useless, but because they are ontic or regional sciences: they study beings already disclosed in some way, while Heidegger asks about the prior intelligibility that lets anything count as a possible object of such a science.

This is why his famous and often misunderstood claim that “science does not think” should not be read simply as anti-scientific obscurantism. The point is that positive sciences operate within pre-given ontological horizons. A psychology that treats humans as organisms, information processors, bundles of drives, conscious subjects, behavioral systems, or neural mechanisms has already accepted a prior interpretation of what a human being is. Heidegger’s question is not “what facts has psychology discovered?” but “what must the being of the human be such that these facts, methods, and pathologies can appear at all?” Spanish-language scholarship on the Zollikon Seminars emphasizes precisely this: Heidegger does not simply reject scientific practice, but interrogates the tacit ontology of the regions studied by psychiatry, psychology, biology, and medicine.

Heidegger’s difference from Husserl is crucial here. Husserl’s phenomenology begins from consciousness and intentional acts; Heidegger radicalizes phenomenology by asking about the being of the entity in which world-disclosure occurs. In that sense, he shifts the center from consciousness to Dasein. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes this contrast clearly: Heidegger does not base philosophy on detached analysis of consciousness, but on the analysis of Dasein as the being through which the question of Being becomes accessible.

2. The anti-Cartesian core: no inner mental chamber

The Cartesian picture Heidegger attacks is not merely Descartes’s explicit metaphysics. It is the broader modern assumption that the human being is first a subject, consciousness, ego, psyche, person, or mind “inside,” and only then somehow related to an external world. Once that assumption is made, the usual problems follow: how does the inner subject reach the outer object? how can the mind know the world? how are other minds inferred? how does the mental relate to the physical? Heidegger’s response is not to solve these problems but to undercut the picture that produces them. In Being and Time, he says that “subject” and “object” do not coincide with Dasein and world, and he exposes the “inner sphere” problem as an artifact of assuming that knowing is first enclosed inside a subject.

The first principle, then, is anti-containment: the mind is not “in” the head or psyche as contents are in a box. This does not mean Heidegger denies brains, nerves, bodies, memories, dreams, or private experiences. It means that these are not the right ontological starting point for understanding mindedness. Dasein is always already outside itself in the sense of being involved with things, people, tasks, dangers, possibilities, and meanings. Thomas Sheehan’s interpretation puts the point sharply: for Heidegger, there is no “inside” to ex-sistence; we are always already exposed to what is other, and intentionality is grounded in this thrown-open relation to meaning.

This is also why Heidegger’s terminology should not be quickly translated into ordinary psychology. Befindlichkeit is not merely a “state of mind,” but disposedness or attunement. Verstehen is not merely intellectual comprehension, but practical-projected understanding. Rede is not merely speech, but the articulation of intelligibility. Sorge is not worry, but care as the structural unity of Dasein’s being. Leib is not the anatomical body as object, but lived bodying. Vorhandenheit and Zuhandenheit are not just two attitudes toward objects, but two modes in which beings are disclosed: objective presence and readiness-to-hand.

3. Being-in-the-world as the replacement for “mind-world relation”

The basic structure of Dasein is being-in-the-world. Heidegger hyphenates the phrase because it is not a composite of a subject plus an external world plus a relation between them. It is a unitary phenomenon. We do not first exist as worldless minds and then acquire world-relations; “taking up relationships towards the world” is possible only because Dasein already is being-in-the-world.

This gives Heidegger’s philosophy of mind an unmistakably ecological or relational character, though “ecological” is not his own technical term. The “world” is not the sum of physical objects. It is an organized field of significance: tools, roles, practices, dangers, promises, obligations, paths, institutions, shared norms, and possibilities. A hammer is not first encountered as an object with properties to which a mental meaning is then added; in ordinary use it shows up as something-for-hammering within a totality of involvements—fastening, building, sheltering, dwelling, and ultimately some possibility of Dasein’s being.

This is the heart of the anti-representational reading of Heidegger. In absorbed practical activity, intelligence is not primarily the manipulation of internal representations. It is skilled coping within an already meaningful situation. Theoretical knowledge, detached observation, explicit belief, and propositional assertion are genuine but derivative. They arise from more primordial forms of involvement, interpretation, and disclosure. This is why Heidegger became important for later debates in philosophy of artificial intelligence and cognitive science, especially through Hubert Dreyfus’s critique of classical symbolic AI; a recent Springer reference entry explicitly notes that Heidegger’s account of coping with tools was used to criticize classical information-processing models.

4. Intentionality: not mental aboutness, but disclosed comportment

In standard philosophy of mind, intentionality is usually the “aboutness” of mental states: beliefs are about facts, desires are about goals, perceptions are about objects. Heidegger does not deny aboutness, but he roots it in something more basic: Dasein’s being already open to a meaningful world. Conscious acts can be about something only because Dasein is already “outside,” already among beings, already responsive to what matters. Sheehan formulates this as “minding the meant,” but with a crucial correction: minding is not a thing inside us; it is the structure of being exposed to meaningful presence.

This means Heideggerian intentionality is practical before it is theoretical. I do not usually encounter the door as a neutral visual object plus an internal representation plus a decision. I simply leave, enter, knock, lock, hold it open for someone, avoid slamming it, or notice that it is stuck. The meaningfulness of the object is in the activity-context. Only when the flow of practice is interrupted does the object become conspicuous as a present-at-hand thing with properties. This is not a denial of perception or cognition; it is a genealogy of them from more basic world-involvement.

So a Heideggerian philosophy of mind should not begin with beliefs and sensations. It should begin with comportments: using, avoiding, awaiting, fearing, promising, dwelling, working, listening, asking, mourning, caring, and being claimed. Mental content is a later abstraction from this field of comportment. This is also why Heidegger is closer to some forms of enactivism, embodied cognition, ecological psychology, and pragmatism than to Cartesian representationalism, though he should not simply be assimilated to any of those later movements.

5. Mood and affect: attunement is disclosure, not inner coloring

Heidegger’s treatment of mood is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind and psychology. Moods are not subjective emotional decorations laid over an otherwise neutral world. They are ways the world is disclosed. Fear, anxiety, boredom, depression, calm, elation, indifference, and serenity are not merely inner affects; they alter what can matter, what solicits action, what threatens, what withdraws, what feels possible, and how one finds oneself. In Being and Time, Heidegger says Dasein always has some mood, and that mood discloses “how one is” before cognition can thematically know it.

This is a major correction to intellectualist accounts of mind. Reason is never moodless. Even scientific objectivity has its own attunement: patience, neutrality, readiness to let something be seen as merely present. Moods are not irrational intrusions into cognition; they are conditions under which anything can be salient at all. A world in anxiety, boredom, grief, manic excitement, depressive heaviness, or technological acceleration is not the same field of affordances as a world in calm practical absorption.

The clinical relevance is direct. A Heideggerian approach to depression, for example, would not reduce the condition to a list of inner symptoms or neurochemical factors, though it need not deny biological and neurochemical factors. Spanish-language work on Heidegger and the Zollikon Seminars stresses that an existential-phenomenological account can complement biological psychiatry by attending to lived body, world-loss, altered significance, fatigue, temporality, and diminished openness.

6. Understanding and agency: Dasein is possibility, not a mental mechanism

Heidegger’s Verstehen, usually translated as understanding, is not primarily conceptual grasp. It is Dasein’s projection into possibilities. To understand a chair is not first to represent its properties; it is to know how to sit, offer, move, repair, sell, avoid, arrange, or ignore it within a world. To understand oneself is not to inspect an inner ego, but to live into possibilities—student, parent, worker, friend, patient, believer, dissenter, thinker, citizen, mortal. Understanding is always mooded, and mood is always understandable; neither pure cognition nor pure affect is fundamental.

Agency, therefore, is not best understood as a mental cause producing bodily motion. It is a motivated, situated response to what matters within a field of possibilities. This does not eliminate causality from nature, but it distinguishes causality from motivation. In the Zollikon Seminars, Heidegger criticizes approaches to psychotherapy that treat human phenomena as causal chains in the manner of natural science; the relevant contrast is between causal determination and being open to claims, reasons, motives, meanings, and possibilities.

The structure that gathers this is care, Sorge. Care does not mean worry, sentiment, or altruistic concern. It names the whole structure of Dasein as ahead-of-itself, already-in-a-world, and alongside beings encountered there. This is Heidegger’s replacement for the ego, the will, the faculty of desire, and the isolated subject of action. The self is not a mental owner standing behind acts; it is the temporal pattern of being involved, thrown, projecting, falling, and taking over possibilities.

7. Selfhood: mineness without ego-substance

Heidegger does not deny first-personality. On the contrary, Dasein is always “mine” in the sense that I have to be it. But mineness is not an ego-substance. It is an existential burden: I cannot outsource the being that I have to live, even when I live it in conformity with what “one” does. This is why Heidegger can reject both impersonal mechanism and Cartesian egoism. Dasein is individuated not by an inner mental core but by the possibilities it takes up, evades, inherits, or owns.

Everyday selfhood is mostly the “they-self” or das Man: the self formed by public norms, shared expectations, common interpretations, and social averageness. This is not merely a sociological claim. It is ontological: Dasein’s world is already public and norm-governed. I normally know how to behave because the world tells me what “one” does: one waits in line, one answers emails, one mourns this way, one dresses that way, one calls this success, one calls that failure. Authenticity is not escaping sociality into a private mind; it is owning one’s finite possibilities rather than being fully absorbed in anonymous normativity.

This gives Heidegger a distinctive answer to the “self” problem. The self is neither a Cartesian ego, nor a Humean bundle, nor a neural self-model, nor a hidden authentic personality. It is the way Dasein is delivered over to possibilities in a world with others, under conditions of finitude, thrownness, and public intelligibility.

8. Other minds: being-with is primordial

Heidegger’s account of sociality is also a philosophy of mind thesis. The problem of other minds arises when one starts from an isolated subject with private experiences and then asks how that subject can infer the existence of other inner subjects from external bodies. Heidegger rejects the starting point. Others are not first encountered as person-things present-at-hand. They are encountered at work, in use-contexts, in shared practices, in concern, in solicitude, in language, in the public world. Dasein is essentially being-with, even when alone.

This means social cognition is not primarily theoretical inference from behavior to hidden mentality. It is a practical attunement to shared situations. I encounter the other as annoyed, hurried, helpful, threatening, absorbed, bored, grieving, waiting, pretending, or avoiding—not by first perceiving bodily movements and then hypothesizing an inner state, but by already inhabiting a meaningful public world. Heidegger does not thereby solve every epistemic problem about deception or misunderstanding, but he denies that our primary relation to others is an inference from outer body to inner mind.

The result is a strong social externalism: mindedness is not private first and social later. The intelligibility of things, roles, emotions, and actions is already shaped by shared practices. Even solitude is a mode of being-with because absence, loneliness, privacy, and withdrawal make sense only against a background of possible others.

9. Language: from articulation to the “house of Being”

In Being and Time, language is rooted in discourse, Rede, which articulates intelligibility before explicit statements. Assertion is derivative from interpretation and understanding; the world is already articulated before we make propositions about it. This matters for philosophy of mind because language is not merely a tool used by an already complete inner subject. It is one way the intelligibility of a world becomes shareable, sayable, and thematically graspable.

In later Heidegger, language becomes even more central. The “turn” in his thought moves away from treating Dasein as the apparent foundation of ontology and toward Being, language, history, art, technology, and the clearing as what grants or withholds intelligibility. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Heidegger’s later work gives language a world-forming power and increasingly treats language and the understanding of Being as essentially interdependent.

This changes the philosophy of mind. Mind is not merely embodied coping plus prelinguistic skill. Human mindedness is historically and linguistically formed. The meanings available to us are not produced by individual consciousness; they are inherited through languages, practices, texts, institutions, artworks, technologies, and historical worlds. Later Heidegger therefore pushes a philosophy of mind toward a philosophy of historical intelligibility.

10. Embodiment: the lived body is not the organism-object

The major criticism of Being and Time is that it says surprisingly little about the body. French reception has been especially important here. Sartre reportedly complained, via Medard Boss, that Being and Time contains very little on the body; later French discussions, including Maxence Caron’s study, treat the issue as central to judging whether Heidegger’s Dasein is too formal, too disembodied, or too indifferent to flesh.

The Zollikon Seminars are the key corrective. There Heidegger distinguishes Körper, the body as corporeal object, from Leib, the lived body. The lived body does not end at the skin the way a physical object does. When I point, reach, hear, gesture, blush, suffer, or sit with someone, the body is not merely a volume in space. Heidegger speaks of “bodying forth” (Leiben): the body is determined by my way of being, and its limits are qualitatively different from the limits of a corporeal thing.

This does not make Heidegger a spiritualist. A French analysis of Heidegger’s body-thinking says his view stands at equal distance from materialism and spiritualism: hearing is not simply located in the ear as a physiological mechanism, but neither is it a purely spiritual act detached from flesh. The relevant phenomenon is embodied, ecstatic openness to a world.

The psychosomatic problem is therefore badly framed if it begins by dividing the human into psyche and soma. Spanish scholarship on Heidegger’s psychiatry dialogues emphasizes that Heidegger and Boss criticize the idea that psyche and soma are two different regions or two interacting substances. The body is not simply somatic, and the psyche is not a closed interior capsule. Human existence is a relational structure of pre-understanding, world-involvement, and shared significance.

11. Soul, spirit, and incorporeal mind

Heidegger has no positive doctrine of an incorporeal mind or immortal soul. His treatment of soul-language is mostly deconstructive. In Being and Time, he explicitly says that the “substance” of the human is not spirit as a synthesis of soul and body, but existence. That is one of the clearest statements relevant to your question. The human being is not a composite of body plus soul plus spirit; such composites presuppose the very metaphysical ontology Heidegger wants to displace.

In the “Letter on Humanism,” Heidegger again criticizes the inherited image of the human as animal rationale, represented as a unity of body, soul, and spirit. He argues that this metaphysical-animal explanation covers over the essence of the human, which he now names ek-sistence in relation to the truth of Being. Language is not simply an added human capacity; it is the “house of Being” in which the human dwells.

Aristotle’s De Anima is important but must be handled carefully. A recent Springer entry on Heidegger and philosophical psychology notes that, for Heidegger, Aristotle’s discussion of the soul is not the modern concept of mind but an inquiry into being-in-the-world. Sheehan similarly notes that Heidegger could interpret Peri Psychēs / De Anima as concerning being-in-the-world; psyche, in this reading, is not a mental closet but the power of being in touch with beings as meaningfully present.

So the answer on incorporeal minds is: Heidegger does not defend them; he dissolves the framework that makes them seem like a natural option. But he is also not a reductive materialist. Dasein is not a ghostly mind, but neither is it merely a biological organism or brain-state. It is the open, finite, embodied, temporal clearing in which beings can appear. If “incorporeal mind” means a self-contained thinking substance independent of world, body, history, and language, Heidegger’s answer is no. If “soul” means the traditional name for the human capacity to be open to beings, then Heidegger translates that capacity into Dasein, care, disclosedness, and ek-sistence rather than into a separate substance.

12. Freud, the unconscious, and psychoanalysis

Heidegger’s relation to psychoanalysis is sharply critical, especially in the Zollikon Seminars. He regards Freud’s metapsychology as captive to a causal-naturalistic model. According to Heidegger’s critique, Freud postulates the unconscious to preserve an unbroken chain of causal explanation for psychical life; Heidegger calls the conscious/unconscious split “fatal” because it encourages treating the human being as a causally determined object.

This does not mean Heidegger thinks all hiddenness is illusion. On the contrary, concealment, fallenness, ambiguity, self-evasion, repression-like avoidance, and unthematized attunement are everywhere in Heidegger. But they are not hidden contents in an inner mental basement. They are ways Dasein is absorbed, covered over, socially interpreted, affectively attuned, and closed off from its own possibilities. A Heideggerian “depth psychology” would therefore become a “breadth” or “world” psychology: it would look at how a person’s world is structured, what can and cannot matter, how time is lived, how the body is inhabited, how others appear, and how language interprets the person in advance.

Italian commentary on the Zollikon Seminars underscores how sweeping Heidegger’s critique is: the usual objectifying representations of psyche, subject, person, ego, and consciousness as capsules must disappear in favor of a different understanding. Another Italian source notes that Heidegger even questions psychoanalytic transfer, because every relation is already affectively attuned from the beginning.

A fair assessment should add that Heidegger may overstate the incompatibility between causal explanation and hermeneutic understanding. A Springer article on psychopathology and causal explanation argues that psychiatric practice need not choose exclusively between natural-scientific causality and interpretive understanding; in practice, both may be needed. That criticism is important because Heidegger’s anti-objectifying impulse can become too rigid if taken to exclude all biological or causal inquiry.

13. Daseinsanalysis and Heidegger’s clinical reception

Heidegger’s most direct engagement with psychology and psychiatry came through Ludwig Binswanger and Medard Boss. Binswanger developed an existential-phenomenological psychiatry influenced by Heidegger, while Boss later worked closely with Heidegger in the Zollikon setting. The official Klostermann page for the German Zollikoner Seminare states that between 1959 and 1969 Boss invited Heidegger to Zollikon eleven times, where Heidegger tested his thoughts on Dasein in direct contact with psychiatrists, psychotherapists, and psychoanalysts.

The International Federation of Daseinsanalysis describes Zurich Daseinsanalysis as a psychotherapy grounded in Heidegger’s ontology, opposed to Cartesian dualism and to reductive natural-scientific or speculative metapsychological accounts. It says Daseinsanalysis proceeds phenomenologically, avoiding causal-genetic interpretations and attending to the meaning-content of phenomena.

This clinical reception is still active. A 2024 German university notice for a volume of the Heidegger Jahrbuch on Heidegger and psychiatry reports publication of correspondence with Binswanger and Viktor Frankl, and describes essays on Heidegger’s influence on psychiatry, psychology, psychotherapy, Daseinsanalysis, health, illness, embodiment, and the doctor-patient relation.

The most responsible conclusion is that Heidegger provides not a therapy manual but an ontological framework for rethinking therapy. Symptoms are not merely inner events; they are modifications of being-in-the-world. Anxiety, depression, compulsion, trauma, mania, bodily dissociation, and despair can be approached as disturbances or transformations of openness, temporality, attunement, embodiment, world-relation, and being-with. That is philosophically powerful, but it should complement rather than replace empirical psychiatry, clinical judgment, and biological care.

14. Animals, life, and non-human minds

A comprehensive Heideggerian philosophy of mind cannot simply become a general theory of cognition in all organisms. Heidegger’s strict analysis of Dasein concerns the being that has an understanding of Being. In Being and Time, life is not treated as the primary category from which Dasein is derived; Heidegger even says life must be interpreted privatively from Dasein’s structure, a claim that already signals his resistance to biological reduction.

In The Fundamental Concepts of Metaphysics, he famously distinguishes the stone as worldless, the animal as poor in world, and the human as world-forming. The animal has an environment and can be affected by things, but, on Heidegger’s account, it does not encounter beings as beings in the full sense of world-disclosure.

This is one of Heidegger’s most controversial positions. It resists both Cartesian mechanism and Darwinian continuity. It grants animals meaningful relational capacities while denying them Dasein’s kind of ontological openness. Modern ethology and animal cognition research may challenge this sharply, but within Heidegger’s own architecture the point is clear: “mind” in the Dasein sense is not equivalent to sentience, intelligence, learning, or adaptive behavior. It is world-disclosive existence.

15. Temporality: mind is not in time; Dasein temporalizes

Heidegger’s account of time is central to any philosophy of mind extracted from him. Human experience is not merely located in clock time. Rather, ordinary clock time is derivative from more primordial temporality: Dasein is ahead of itself in projection, already thrown into a past, and presently absorbed among beings. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Heidegger’s view as an account in which human actions and experiences are temporally structured by retention of the past and awaiting of the future, and where primordial temporality makes ordinary world-time possible.

This means memory, anticipation, attention, habit, anxiety, regret, hope, boredom, and agency are not separate mental modules. They are modes of ecstatic temporality. The mind is not a container that happens to have temporal contents; Dasein is temporal through and through. This is especially important for psychopathology. Aho’s work on acceleration and time pathologies, drawing on Heidegger, argues that technological acceleration can fragment the self, overwhelm affective life, and produce cultural moods of indifference or profound boredom; he also criticizes mainstream psychology when it ignores the socio-historical temporality within which distress is produced.

16. Later Heidegger: technology, history, and the limits of a Dasein-centered philosophy of mind

The later Heidegger complicates any simple extraction of a philosophy of mind from Being and Time. After the “turn,” he increasingly thinks not from Dasein as the apparent transcendental center, but from Being, language, Ereignis, history, technology, art, dwelling, and the clearing. This does not abolish the early analytic, but it changes its status. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes both continuity and discontinuity: Heidegger remains relationalist, but increasingly treats being as historical and the clearing as something received rather than produced by human projection.

For philosophy of mind, this means that mindedness is not merely individual embodiment or first-person openness. It is historically configured. What can count as real, normal, pathological, rational, efficient, measurable, spiritual, bodily, or psychological depends on an epochal clearing. Modern psychology itself may be part of the technological age’s tendency to render beings measurable, calculable, and controllable. That does not invalidate psychology, but it means psychology must ask whether its methods disclose the human being or partially conceal it.

17. The reconstructed Heideggerian philosophy of mind

A rigorous reconstruction would look like this.

First, the object of inquiry is not mind but Dasein. “Mind” is a derivative abstraction from being-in-the-world. The basic phenomenon is not internal consciousness but disclosed existence.

Second, intentionality is grounded in openness. Mental aboutness depends on Dasein’s being already outside itself among meaningful beings. Representation is derivative from practical comportment.

Third, perception is world-involved and embodied. We do not receive neutral sensory data and infer objects. We encounter usable, threatening, inviting, familiar, strange, public, and bodily accessible things in a meaningful field.

Fourth, affect is ontological. Moods disclose the world as mattering in some way. They are neither merely subjective nor merely physiological, though they can have physiological dimensions.

Fifth, understanding is projection into possibility. Agency is not a mental cause added to bodily motion; it is motivated comportment within a field of possible ways to be.

Sixth, the self is care, not ego-substance. Selfhood is mineness, thrownness, projection, fallenness, and possible authenticity. There is no hidden Cartesian owner behind experience.

Seventh, sociality is primordial. Other minds are not inferred from bodies; Dasein is being-with, and the public world already structures intelligibility.

Eighth, language articulates and historically shapes intelligibility. In early Heidegger, language is rooted in discourse; in later Heidegger, language becomes world-forming and bound to the history of Being.

Ninth, the body is lived bodying, not merely organism-object. The mind-body problem is transformed because the human being is not a psyche plus soma but embodied being-in-the-world.

Tenth, soul and spirit are de-substantialized. Heidegger does not defend incorporeal minds. He interprets inherited soul-language as a metaphysical way of gesturing toward openness, but replaces it with Dasein, ek-sistence, and the clearing.

Eleventh, psychopathology is a modification of being-in-the-world. Mental suffering is not merely an inner malfunction; it is altered attunement, temporality, embodiment, world-relation, and being-with. This does not exclude biology, but it denies that biology alone gives the phenomenon.

Twelfth, natural science is legitimate but ontologically partial. Neuroscience, psychiatry, and psychology can discover real causal structures, but they presuppose a prior understanding of what counts as human, mental, bodily, normal, pathological, and measurable.

18. Critical cautions

There are real weaknesses and dangers in using Heidegger this way. The first is the body problem. Being and Time leaves embodiment underdeveloped, and the Zollikon Seminars are fragmentary rather than systematic. Compared with Merleau-Ponty, Heidegger gives less sustained phenomenological description of perception, sexuality, motor intentionality, illness, disability, and developmental embodiment. French and later body-focused scholarship has rightly made this a major issue.

The second is the risk of anti-naturalistic exaggeration. Heidegger is strongest when he shows that causal explanation does not exhaust human meaning. He is weaker if read as implying that causality has no place in psychiatry or psychology. Good clinical practice often needs both causal explanation and existential understanding.

The third is the political and ethical problem. Heidegger’s Nazism and antisemitism are not incidental biographical trivia. The Stanford Encyclopedia states that his support for National Socialism and use of Nazi and antisemitic tropes are well documented, and warns that scholars should remain alert to possible distortions in his accounts of history, technology, peoplehood, and world-disclosure. This matters for philosophy of mind because any account that emphasizes historical worlds, communal moods, destiny, and shared disclosure can become politically dangerous if tied to ethnonational or exclusionary categories.

19. Final synthesis

Heidegger’s philosophy of mind, if extracted carefully, is a philosophy of disclosive being-in-the-world. It rejects the picture of the human as a mental interior attached to a body and looking out at an external world. It also rejects the opposite reduction of the human to a physical organism described only by causal science. Between these, Heidegger gives a third model: the human being is finite, embodied, attuned, practical, social, linguistic, historical openness.

That makes his position anti-Cartesian, anti-representationalist, anti-reductionist, anti-dualist, and anti-psychologistic. But it is not anti-body, anti-science, or anti-reality when read carefully. The brain, body, drives, symptoms, and causal mechanisms are real, but they are ontic regions disclosed within a more basic existential field. The “soul,” if the word is retained at all, is not an incorporeal substance; it is a historically inherited name for the openness Heidegger rethinks as Dasein, care, ek-sistence, and clearing.

A compact formula would be: for Heidegger, mind is not something we have inside us; it is the way we are opened, claimed, attuned, embodied, and involved in a meaningful world.

PreviousEdmund Husserl NextJean-Paul Sartre

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
